title: NEEDLE DEPTH: How to choose best depth to avoid complications & get results [Aesthetic Mastery Show]
filename: NEEDLE DEPTH： How to choose best depth to avoid complications & get results [Aesthetic Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: What's the best depth of injection to use what are the pros and the cons what are the reasons why sometimes we're deep sometimes we're superficial and sometimes we're in between and that's what we're covering on today's show I'm Dr Tim Pierce hi I'm Miranda Pearce and this is the…

segments.text: what's the best depth of injection to use what are the pros and the cons what are the reasons why sometimes we're deep sometimes we're superficial and sometimes we're in between and that's what we're covering on today's show I'm Dr Tim Pierce hi I'm Miranda Pearce and this is the Aesthetics Mastery show [Music] s that are involved in making the depth decision so before I answer that can you give us a like if you're excited to learn with us today on the show we really appreciate the support so um the way I've broken it down is I think there are four factors that dictate uh the depth that we should be injecting it and obviously that varies on your intention but the first is what are the aesthetic indications what is it that you're trying to achieve are you trying to remove a wrinkle are you trying to create some definition are you trying to create a curvature are we removing a shadow all of those things play a role in deciding what depth you

need to inject and of course you can see that quite obviously with the crease you're going to be more superficial but maybe with a contour you're going to be deeper so that's the aesthetic indication the next is the complication risk so there are parts of the face where you've got structures underneath where you wouldn't dream of sticking a needle so if you're right above a nerve plexus you're not going to stick a needle onto the bone because you're going to hit that collection of nerves likewise if you're near an entry point of an art of the artery into the face so you might be near the infra orbital artery or perhaps the facial artery as it enters over the angle of the jaw these are areas where going deep onto the periosteum would be very dangerous and that would be a reason why you would never go deep in those areas we also have the aesthetic indication in terms of how it moves so if you're at different levels you'll find that the movement of the face changes how the filler interacts over time so if you think about trying to emulate bone for example so typical example would be maybe the angle of the jaw so if you're

injecting superficially trying to create a shape that looks like an angle of the jaw but you're in The Superficial fat you will find that as the as your mouth opens the filler moves doesn't move with the bone and then you create an odd look where you can see the shape sitting on top of the structure as it moves so it's actually a little bit like I have with my beard because I shave along the jawline if I open my mouth you'll see that the head suddenly doesn't fit my jawline anymore because the jawline's move now they're exactly the same thing can happen with filler along the jawline so that might be a reason why we choose to inject along the periosteum where possible as long as we're not near the facial artery of course and the next indication we need to think about is how efficiently will the job be done because I've discovered over time that different levels of injection you get a different bang for your buck and sometimes we're trying to be efficient with product it may be better to be more superficial for example and similarly intermediate depth you may lose some of that product and sometimes it's better to be deeper if you're trying to emulate the structure of bone so these are the four factors so

in summary there are four key factors that control the depth at which we inject there is how does it look because that's our aesthetic indication how does it move so that can also change depending on depth and how efficient is your product getting the job done and finally what are the complications that you're risking at different depths so what's the best way to break this down so I thought what we could do is work through the different layers of the skin and then we'll talk about the indications for injecting at each layer and then the advantages disadvantages and of course the risks at each layer so presumably we're starting with the deadness at the top yeah so injecting the dermis is one of the more common things we do obviously because lines and wrinkles are basically that problem is most obvious at the level of the dermis and if you're going to try and correct a line or wrinkle it's worth understanding the anatomy of that wrinkle and essentially what happens is your start by simply folding the dermis but over time you actually get a decrease in thickness of the reticular layer of the dermis and so the top layer of the skin

gets closer to the layer beneath sometimes you can actually see this if you look at some of the deepest wrinkles and pull them apart you can actually see a often a slightly pink line at the bottom of that and that is showing you that underneath the wrinkle you've actually got a breakdown of the tissue it's a much thinner component of the skin and that's what we're trying to do when we're injecting in the dermises we're strengthening the skin to prevent the later or subsequent breakdown and thinning of the reticular layer of the dermis so it's a preventative but you do need to stop the skin bending at that point so that's probably the main indication for injecting in the mid to superficial dermis but there are also other times when you might inject there as well because it's the layer where you get the most amount of traction superficially so by traction I mean support if you imagine bunching together skin if you often I do it when I'm examining a patient you give a little squeeze and you see with a little the little weak points form there's like a ripple in the surface if you inject in the dermis you can resist that and that is probably the main indication for injecting at that layer is that it

resists gravity and the other Force across the face really well at that level of the skin can you give us the kind of like areas of the face that might be any line or wrinkle you might inject there so it could mean a metallabial fold marionette lines or the nasolabial fold if there's a crease you're going to be superficial now if you're just treating a shadow that's not going to work so that's a very big important distinction about the level of injection is that when you're injecting what's a contour or Shadow like nasolabial folds quite often are there is no point injecting superficially if there is no actual crease so the indication is most often creases and but you're also that sort of depth if you're trying to create maybe definitions somewhere particularly the Vermilion border you're also equally very superficial at that point so that there's similar types of layers that you might inject but it's basically any line or wrinkle this is what are the advantages of injecting here so if you're a superficial you know where you are so you can do a little depth check it's a very clear simple depth

check which you elevate the needle don't seesaw it because that hurts the patient but you lift everything up um parallel with the surface of the skin and you should see the skin Blanche and that will tell you that you're underneath the papillary dermis but your superficial enough that you're above most of the hypodermis the fat and that is a good position to push the skin out support the skin and decrease the um the chance of it bending soften a wrinkle and that's the advantage of injecting at that level you know where you are it's usually not that close to most blood vessels so it's a safer place to inject and it's an effective place to eject as long as you're injecting for the right reasons disadvantages probably your next question is that you're superficial enough that if you use a product that's too thick you might see it so lumps bumps um basically it's not going to hide any mistakes for you if you put a bit too much in you're going to see it you know the most critical air area would be in the tear trough so if you're injecting there the dermis is so thin a little bit too much you'll see a slug of the filler

and that causes an aesthetic complication you can see the problem so that's the disadvantage of that area in terms of the risks it is it does tend to be safer in general um but you can be if you're too superficial and this is just my theory but I think if you're above the papillary dermis that's where the blood supply Peaks you know it's the highest point of the blood supply supplying the epidermis you could cause a pressure type necrosis so this is a very thin area of necrosis that basically just I've seen a couple of these over the years through the training Network that we support where you get a line of filler that follows the shape of the needle that then causes a small breakdown in tissue which normally doesn't scar but it is a superficial type of necrosis which I think is akin to a pressure sore from compressing the capillaries on the papillary dermis and that's from being a bit too superficial the way that you know about that is that you sh when you're injecting before you do your depth check you see a gray color to the needle you've already squeezed the blood out of the tissue and it doesn't matter

how neutral your Force across the needle is you see that white area of the needle you're basically seeing the color of the needle with no blood above it and that is too superficial what about the Kindle effect yeah so that would also be something that you get as well so um if you injected that level you're above most of what would hide the filler and injecting too superficially or too much in the superficial plane will cause internal effects all the single effect is basically that the filler refracts light differently to skin and you you're just seeing the slight blue tint that you get with almost all fillers but some are worse than others um so that you're actually seeing the filler instead of seeing tissue the problems you're describing with the being too superficial do they apply to all fillers so the firmer your product the higher the G Prime the less tolerant is going to be of a superficial injection and that what that means is you'll be able to see the shape of the product because um what a very low viscosity product or an uncrossed link product just basically oozes between all those retinacular

fibers and it's and it forms a nice sheet whereas this if it's thicker it holds itself together more and you'll see the shape now um with intermediate thickness fillers and this happened to me when I first started injecting as I was told you can use them for both those lines and with a good experienced hand you can but you know when you're a new injector you tend to basically have a little bit less finesse and I remember um finding a couple of patients being able to see not when they were static but when they would do certain movements they would perceive that superficial nature of it so we have to use products that are very good for superficial lines where the manufacturer recommends using them at that level or if you've got five years of experience and you're good at spreading that there's a bit of leeway but as a general rule and we use the exactly the product that the manufacturer recommends so what we've covered there is the dermis the papillary dermis and the retinacular cutus those are the top three layers uh what's the next layer hypodermis very good so hypodermis what are the advantages of injecting in the

hypodermis so this is the an easier plane to pass things like a cannula you you've simply effectively can't inject in the derms with a canola you'll always scoop beneath so a lot of the kind of Shadows maybe some of the curvatures we're correcting when you're more superficial is in the hypodermis good things about injecting here is you're probably if you're with a cannula you're going to be slightly above the arteries but you're getting much closer to them um and you're going to have an easier path with a cannula than when you're deeper so those are the main advantages the indications would be really that you'd you're normally treating curvatures or adding a little bit of additional structure it's not the main place that you're providing structure and Contour that's usually deeper but you can do it and probably the most common reason I injectors because you're too afraid of what's underneath so it's jawline work when you when you're above for example the facial artery you're unlikely to be injecting deep over there same with underneath the zygoma if you're ever

filling that area you tend to be more superficial in the hypodermis any risks of injecting the hypogenesis so there are certain parts of the face with the arteries tend to gravitate to intermediate layers so the most common that you're likely to come across would be the facial artery when you're injecting the nasolabial fold it is quite often on the deep side of the hypodermis and that means if you're injecting over here and you're in at intermediate depth then obviously you may cause a vascular occlusion so that's why a lot of people use the Deep periosteal injection in at that position and so that's the main one I think about but it's normally most of the arteries are not you know if you're injecting in most areas you've got to be on the deeper side anyway um but you'd be thinking talking along those lines at certain areas of the face when you're injecting so I think we have the smart snacks next yes so the smash is a very important layer for supporting structures now we never actually directly try and inject into this Mass but we're often you thinking about are we above all beneath it so as a rough rule of thumb Above This Mass hypodermis beneath the smash is the Deep Fat pads

and it's very important if you're thinking about lifting using the ligaments so particularly with cheek treatments you want to be underneath the smash a lot of the treatments that we do where you're trying to create structure are indirectly lifting tissue and like we've discussed in our ligament video which we'll link down below that when you support the ligaments by injecting beneath the smash you have a lifting effect in theory and that's the main thing to know about this Mass which is that it's you're going to be better at lifting if you're beneath this mass and especially with the knowledge of ligaments you should be directly thinking about those structures when you're adding volume at that level so generally with this Mass you're injecting below it which you need to be aware of it tell us about the advantages of the Deep Fat pads the next one down so the main advantages of injecting in the deep fat pad while relating to the smash also is it's get it's a much more forgiving place to inject aesthetically because you're under you're covered by this Mass so if you're in the deep fat pad and you create a little bit of projection

um you're not going to see the shapes and Contours of the individual Blobs of filler like you're just going to see a general lift in that direction so it's aesthetically very forgiving um another really key Advantage is for many of the blood vessels what we're often doing is injecting on the periosteum with a needle um and that means that you're further away in certain places most of the face in fact you're further away from the arteries so long as you aren't near the foramen where the arteries enter the face and if you're injecting in the deep fat pad that is a nice place to inject in terms of decrease in Risk from vascular occlusion but also this that is a good place to create contouring which is what we're doing mostly for chin jawline cheek treatments and finally the bottom the bone the periods team what are the advantages of ejecting here so I mean the nice thing about the if you're intending to inject on the periosteum is you know exactly when you're at that level because you actually feel the needle touch it and that has is a very reassuring place to inject in certain areas of the face and a very risky place

it's well a scary place that you wouldn't inject when you know your Anatomy if you're in the wrong place so um it's essentially um the most stable place to inject as well so I think aesthetically so jaw lines are an excellent example of this and I've now seen a case come up on our complications group where someone who did cannula with jawline which is very common the patient complained that when she moved as we've already discussed that she could see the filler sitting on top as she opened her mouth and she didn't like that effect when she's still in a photograph she looks great but the movement revealed the depth of the product so that isn't a reason to inject on the periosteum particularly with chins and jaw lines if you want the movement to look natural injecting on the periosteum is probably a much better place to inject but of course you've got another and avoid the facial artery but that that's probably one of the biggest benefits is I think you create a more stable structure everything else sits on top of it and you don't have this movement so if you think about what's happening with all the fat pads as we covered in our ligament video

they're sliding on top of each other in this structure and the more superficial you get even in the deep fat pad if you're the top end of it the filler is going to be more mobile so the deeper you get the Stiller it should be in most movements and therefore maybe the longer it lasts the less chance of migration and I think that's probably the most important thing to know about injecting on the periosteum is in terms of that stability long term we also have a great video on where not to inject deep so we'll link that in the description as well yeah and that's probably the biggest risk to know is you need to know your entry points are five entry points of arteries into the face and they're basically center around mainly the foramen so you're super trochlia super orbital uh infraorbital mental um what are the others facial artery um all of those entry points as the artery comes into the face it tends to be on the bone and obviously those are the places where you would never inject on the periosteum so how are we supposed to know how deep we are when we can't

see so depth check is the most important thing you can do so um now you've got to know a little bit about the type of skin you're injecting so there's big differences in the thickness of the skin if you're injecting the tear trough firstly there are fewer layers or three layers in the Tetra for example but also it's only 0.2 millimeters thick the dermis so it can look like you're very superficial but you are still under the dermis you're not in the epidermis for example even though you're so superficial because the skin is thinner so you need to know the skin and there's different thicknesses so chins are very thick the LaBella is a very thick place to inject and that might change what you see but essentially it's about depth check um that blanching thing which I described earlier when you see that you see it Blanche if you want to be in the hypodermis um then when you lift you should just see all the tissue left but not a clear line of blanching in most of the face so typical would be you know a crease on your cheek for example um if you want to be inflating the volume of a superficial cheek fat

pad a little bit it and when you lift up it shouldn't be clear where the needle is you should just see the shape of the needle and maybe slight blanching but it shouldn't be clear at all when you're more superficial much clearer where the needle is and where the blanching is and if you're too superficial there's continuous blanching it doesn't change at all no matter what you do with your needle it's white but it's also very small area of white just basically following the shape of the needle and of course if you're on the periosteum you know that because you can feel it it's hard so I hope that helped you refresh some of the concepts and your Anatomy if you want to download a free guide to where not to inject too deeply we have that linked down below and don't forget to like if you found that helpful and subscribe to the channel and we will see you next week take care bye [Music] foreign
